sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking on [54, 14] (top-right corner of id=12) moved it from [54-55, 13-14] to [53-54, 13-14]. So it moved up by 1 row but stayed in the same column. This is different from the up-left movement!
Wait, let me check: id=12 had boundary [54-55, 13-14], and after clicking [54, 14], it moved to [53-54, 13-14]. So it moved up by 1 row, but the column stayed the same (13-14).
So clicking on the top-right corner moves the square up (not up-left).
Let me summarize the movement mechanics:
- Clicking top-left corner: moves up-left (row-1, col-1)
- Clicking top-right corner: moves up (row-1, col+0)
- Clicking bottom-right corner: no move (tested earlier)
Let me test clicking on the bottom-left corner to see if it moves left.
Actually, I should also test clicking on the center cells to see what happens.
But first, let me think about the goal. If I can move the squares in different directions by clicking on different corners, then I can move them to any position. The goal is likely to move them to specific target positions.
Let me think about what the target positions might be. The blue blob is at [23-31, 29-37]. Maybe the magenta squares need to be moved to surround the blue blob.
Actually, let me test the bottom-left corner first to understand the full movement mechanics.

<parameter=code>
# Test clicking on bottom-left corner of id=12 at [54, 13]
predict(expect_change=True, note='Clicking bottom-left [54,13] of magenta square - test if moves left')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 54, 'col': 13}])
print("Result:", last_action_result)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Magenta nodes after click:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
